parts.homeLangDetails: A Finnish suffix forming place names — a location associated with the base noun. The same suffix appears in words like apteekki (pharmacy) and varasto (storage). Think of it as '-ery' or '-arium' in English.

words.exampleTranslation: I borrow a book from the library.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Finnish -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something' — much like English '-ing' in 'reading' or 'writing'. It's one of the most productive nominalizing suffixes in Finnish.

words.exampleTranslation: Reading is important to me.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Finnish -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something' — like English '-ing'. Here kirjoittaa (to write) becomes kirjoittaminen (writing).

words.exampleTranslation: Writing helps me think more clearly.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Finnish -ja/-jä suffix forms agent nouns — a person who does the action. Opettaa means 'to teach', so opettaja is 'one who teaches'. The same pattern appears in laulaja (singer, from laulaa, to sing) and juoksija (runner, from juosta, to run).

words.exampleTranslation: The teacher explained the assignment carefully.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Finnish agent suffix -ja/-jä, meaning 'one who does something'. Opiskella means 'to study', so opiskelija is 'one who studies'. The same suffix appears in opettaja (teacher) and myyjä (seller).

words.exampleSentence: Opiskelija luki tenttiä varten koko yön.
words.exampleTranslation: The student read all night for the exam.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Finnish suffix -la/-lä forms place nouns — a location associated with the base word. Ravinto means 'nourishment' or 'food', so ravintola is literally 'a place of nourishment'. The same suffix appears in koulu (school → koulula wouldn't be standard, but the pattern is visible in words like sairaala).

words.exampleTranslation: We ate dinner at an Italian restaurant.
Helsinki is the largest city in Finland.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Finnish suffix -lainen/-läinen forms words meaning 'a person associated with or belonging to' the base noun. Kaupunki means 'city', so kaupunkilainen is 'a city person' or 'city dweller'. The same suffix is used for nationalities and origins: suomalainen (Finnish person), helsinkiläinen (person from Helsinki).

words.exampleTranslation: He is a born-and-bred city dweller.

parts.homeLangDetails: From sairas (sick, ill). The stem saira- connects to illness and disease throughout Finnish vocabulary, as in sairaanhoitaja (nurse) and sairaus (illness).

A variant of the Finnish place-forming suffix -la/-lä. Here it attaches to the sickness root to mean 'a place for the sick'. The same suffix pattern appears in ravintola (restaurant, place of nourishment).

words.exampleTranslation: He was taken to the hospital by ambulance.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of sairas (sick, ill). In Finnish compounds, the genitive case is often used to show that one noun belongs to or relates to another. So sairaan- here means 'of the sick person' — the one being cared for.
The Finnish agent suffix -ja/-jä, meaning 'one who does the action'. Hoitaa means 'to care for', so hoitaja is 'caregiver'. Combined with sairaan-, the whole word means 'one who cares for the sick'.

words.exampleTranslation: The nurse measured the patient's blood pressure.
The doctor wrote me a prescription.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of lääkäri (doctor). In Finnish, the genitive (-n) is used to show possession or association within a compound or phrase. Lääkärin means 'of the doctor' or 'doctor's'.

In isolation, vastaan means 'against' or 'towards'. But in this compound it contributes the idea of 'receiving' or 'meeting' — the doctor comes toward (meets) the patient. It's the same root as in vastaanottaa (to receive, to accept).

From ottaa (to take). Otto here means 'a taking' or 'reception'. Combined with vastaan-, vastaanotto means 'reception' or 'receiving'. A doctor's vastaanotto is the appointment or consulting session where the doctor 'receives' the patient.

words.translationLong: doctor's appointment, doctor's office visit, medical consultation
words.exampleTranslation: I have a doctor's appointment tomorrow morning.
I bought a new novel from the bookstore.
I go to the grocery store every week.

words.exampleSentence: Tietokoneeni on rikki, joten en pysty tekemään töitä.
words.exampleTranslation: My computer is broken, so I can't work.

parts.homeLangDetails: From puhua (to speak) + -lin, a suffix for instruments or devices. So a puhelin is literally 'a speaking device.'

words.exampleTranslation: I left my mobile phone at home.

parts.homeLangDetails: From puhua (to speak) + -lin, a suffix for instruments or devices. Literally 'a speaking device.'

words.exampleTranslation: Can you give me your phone number?
We'll meet at the train station at three o'clock.
The airport is outside the city.
The bus station is near the market square.

parts.homeLangDetails: The verbal stem of uida (to swim) in its nominal/compound form. It shows up in many compound words related to swimming, like uimapuku (swimsuit) and uimaranta (swimming beach).

words.exampleTranslation: The children are playing in the swimming pool.
The bookshelf is full of novels.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of ruoka (food). In Finnish compounds, the first element often appears in the genitive, indicating that something belongs to or concerns it. Here it signals that this is cooking that belongs to food — the preparation of food.
From laittaa (to put, to prepare, to make). Laitto as a noun refers to the act of making or putting something together. In this compound it conveys preparation — putting food together.

words.exampleTranslation: Cooking is relaxing for me.

words.flag: Possible misspelling — standard Finnish is 'ruoanlaittaja' (cook, noun) or 'ruoanlaitto' (cooking). Please verify the intended word.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of ruoka (food). In Finnish compounds, the first element often appears in the genitive to show that something relates to it.

Appears to be a truncated or colloquial form related to laittaa (to prepare, to make). The full agent noun would be laittaja (one who makes/prepares). As written, this may be an informal shortening or a typo.

words.translationLong: Likely intended as 'cook' (person) — the standard form is ruoanlaittaja
words.exampleTranslation: He is a skilled cook.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something.' It's one of the most productive suffixes in Finnish — juoda (to drink) becomes juominen (drinking), just as syödä (to eat) becomes syöminen (eating).

words.exampleTranslation: Drinking enough is important for your health.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something.' Syödä (to eat) becomes syöminen (eating).

words.exampleTranslation: Eating slowly helps digestion.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something.' Nukkua (to sleep) becomes nukkuminen (sleeping).

words.exampleTranslation: Good sleep improves your mood.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something.' Herätä (to wake up) becomes herääminen (waking up).

words.exampleTranslation: Waking up early is difficult for me.

parts.homeLangDetails: From harjoitella (to practice, to train), which is itself a frequentative form of harjoittaa (to exercise, to practice something). The frequentative ending -le- conveys repeated or ongoing action.
A frequentative suffix indicating that the action is repeated or habitual. Harjoitella, with -le-, emphasizes ongoing or regular practice rather than a one-time act.
The -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun meaning 'the act of doing something.' Harjoitella (to practice) becomes harjoitteleminen (practicing).

words.exampleTranslation: Regular practice develops skills.

parts.homeLangDetails: From liikkua (to move). Liikunta refers specifically to physical exercise or movement as a beneficial activity — the same root gives liike (movement, motion) and liikenne (traffic).

words.exampleTranslation: I go to the fitness center three times a week.
I play soccer on weekends.
She is good at basketball.
Ice hockey is Finland's national sport.
Today is a cold winter day.
We enjoyed a warm summer day.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the genitive form of 'aurinko' (sun), meaning 'of the sun'. Finnish compounds often use the genitive to link elements, so 'auringon' simply marks the sun as the possessor — 'the sun's setting'.
From 'laskea' (to lower, to descend, to set). In everyday Finnish it also means 'bill' or 'calculation', but here it captures the sun going down — a lowering or descent.

words.exampleTranslation: The sunset was magnificent yesterday evening.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of 'aurinko' (sun), meaning 'of the sun'. Finnish compounds frequently use the genitive to link elements, expressing a possessive or descriptive relationship between parts.
From 'nousta' (to rise, to ascend, to go up). The same root appears in words like 'ylösnousu' (resurrection) and 'hinnan nousu' (price increase) — it always carries the sense of going upward.

words.exampleTranslation: I woke up early to see the sunrise.

parts.homeLangDetails: In isolation 'sade' means rain specifically, but as the second element of weather compounds it broadens to mean any kind of falling precipitation. So 'lumisade' is 'snow-fall' and 'vesisade' is 'rain-fall' (water-fall in the weather sense).

words.exampleTranslation: The snowfall surprised us in the morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: Normally 'sade' alone means rain, so 'vesisade' is a bit like saying 'water-rain' — emphasizing that it is liquid precipitation as opposed to snow or sleet. The compound is used to distinguish from other types of sade (like lumisade or räntäsade).

words.exampleTranslation: The rain continued all day.

parts.homeLangDetails: A very common Finnish adjectival suffix that attaches to nouns to mean 'having the quality of' or 'full of' — like English -y or -ful. You see it all over weather words: pilvinen (cloudy), aurinkoinen (sunny), luminen (snowy).

words.exampleTranslation: Today the weather is very windy.

parts.homeLangDetails: A very common Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'having the quality of' or 'full of'. It turns nouns into adjectives the way English -y does: pilvi (cloud) → pilvinen (cloudy), tuuli (wind) → tuulinen (windy).

words.exampleTranslation: The sky is very cloudy today.

parts.homeLangDetails: The same adjectival suffix as -nen (the vowel shifts slightly after certain stems). It means 'having the quality of' — so aurinkoinen is literally 'sun-ful', i.e. sunny. Compare pilvinen (cloudy) and tuulinen (windy).

words.exampleTranslation: Tomorrow will be a sunny day.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Finnish nominalizing suffix that turns adjectives into abstract nouns, just like English -ness. Kylmä (cold) → kylmyys (coldness). The double vowel is typical of this suffix's long-vowel form. Compare kuumuus (heat) from kuuma (hot).

words.exampleTranslation: The cold was felt to the bone.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Finnish nominalizing suffix turning adjectives into abstract nouns, like English -ness. Kuuma (hot) → kuumuus (hotness, heat). The suffix appears as -yys after front vowels (kylmyys) and -uus after back vowels (kuumuus) due to vowel harmony.

words.exampleTranslation: The heat of summer was unbearable.

parts.homeLangDetails: On its own, 'tila' means 'space', 'room', or 'state/condition'. In this compound it carries the sense of a measurable condition or state — so lämpötila is literally the 'state of warmth', i.e. temperature. The same word appears in 'tilanne' (situation) and 'tila' (farm, space).

words.exampleTranslation: What is today's temperature?

parts.homeLangDetails: The root of ystävä (friend). The final -ä shifts to -äv- when suffixes are added.

A Finnish derivational suffix that turns adjectives or nouns into abstract nouns — similar to English '-ness' or '-ship'. It follows vowel harmony, appearing as -uus/-yys depending on the vowels in the root.

words.exampleTranslation: Their friendship has lasted for decades.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root of ystävä (friend). The final -ä shifts to -äv- when suffixes are added.

A Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'having the quality of' or 'characteristic of', roughly equivalent to English '-ly' or '-like' when applied to nouns. It follows vowel harmony, appearing as -allinen/-ällinen.

words.exampleSentence: Hän on aina ollut hyvin ystävällinen naapureitaan kohtaan.
words.exampleTranslation: She has always been very friendly toward her neighbors.

parts.homeLangDetails: Viha means hatred, anger, or hostility — a core Finnish noun. It appears in compounds and derivatives related to aggression or enmity.
From mieli (mind, spirit, mood). This root appears in many Finnish compound words relating to mental state or disposition, much like English '-minded'.
A very common Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'full of' or 'having the quality of', similar to English '-ful', '-ous', or '-ish'. It follows vowel harmony, appearing as -inen/-inen.

words.exampleSentence: Miksi olet niin vihamielinen minua kohtaan?
words.exampleTranslation: Why are you so hostile toward me?

parts.homeLangDetails: Suru is the Finnish word for sorrow, grief, or sadness — a core emotional noun used widely in everyday speech and literature.
A variant of the -inen adjectival suffix, appearing as -llinen after vowel-final stems to mean 'characterized by' or 'full of'. Equivalent to English '-ful' or '-ous'.

words.exampleTranslation: The child looked sad after losing their toy.

parts.homeLangDetails: Ilo is the Finnish word for joy, happiness, or delight — a foundational positive emotion noun.
A common Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'full of' or 'having the quality of', similar to English '-ful' or '-ous'.

words.exampleTranslation: She was happy when she heard the news.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the verb väsyä (to get tired, to become fatigued). This root captures the process of growing weary.

The Finnish active past participle suffix -nut/-nyt, which follows vowel harmony. It turns a verb into an adjective or participial form meaning 'having [done]' — so väsynyt literally means 'having become tired', i.e., tired.

words.exampleSentence: Olin niin väsynyt pitkän päivän jälkeen.
words.exampleTranslation: I was so tired after the long day.

parts.homeLangDetails: Borrowed from the international/European word for energy (Greek energeia via Latin/Swedish). The Finnish form drops the final vowel of the base form energía before the suffix.
A variant of the Finnish adjectival suffix -inen/-nen meaning 'having the quality of', here adapted to a loanword stem.

words.exampleTranslation: He is a very energetic person.

parts.homeLangDetails: A single-root adjective with no transparent morpheme split in modern Finnish. The word is ancient and not productively decomposable into separate meaningful parts today.

words.exampleTranslation: The sunset was truly beautiful.

parts.homeLangDetails: A single-root adjective meaning ugly or unattractive. It is not transparently decomposable into smaller meaningful parts in modern Finnish.

words.exampleSentence: Hän sanoi, että taulussa on ruma väri.
words.exampleTranslation: She said the painting has an ugly color.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the adjective nopea (fast, quick, rapid). The stem shifts to nope- before the abstract noun suffix.
The Finnish abstract noun suffix -uus/-us/-yys, which derives nouns of quality from adjectives, much like English '-ness' or '-ity'. Here it turns 'fast' into 'speed/fastness'.

words.exampleSentence: Auton nopeus oli liian suuri kaupungissa.
words.exampleTranslation: The car's speed was too high in the city.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the adjective hidas (slow). The stem form hita- appears before the abstract noun suffix.
The Finnish abstract noun suffix -uus/-us, deriving nouns of quality from adjectives. Here it turns 'slow' into 'slowness'.

words.exampleTranslation: The slowness of the project began to frustrate the team.

parts.homeLangDetails: Voima means strength, power, or force — a core Finnish noun that also appears in words like voimistella (to exercise) and ydinvoima (nuclear power).
The Finnish adjectival suffix -kas/-käs, meaning 'having plenty of' or 'characterized by'. It is similar to '-ful' or '-ous' in English, and always follows vowel harmony.

words.exampleTranslation: The wind was very strong on the coast.

parts.homeLangDetails: A single-root adjective meaning weak, feeble, or frail. It is not transparently decomposable into smaller meaningful parts in modern Finnish.

words.exampleSentence: Signaali oli niin heikko, että puhelu katkesi.
words.exampleTranslation: The signal was so weak that the call dropped.

parts.homeLangDetails: A single-root adjective meaning clean or pure. Related to the verb puhdistaa (to clean, to purify), but the adjective itself is not transparently decomposable in modern Finnish.

words.exampleTranslation: The water was crystal clear and pure.

parts.homeLangDetails: Lika means dirt, filth, or grime — the base noun from which this adjective is formed.
A common Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'full of' or 'covered with', similar to English '-y' (as in 'dirty') or '-ful'. It follows vowel harmony.

words.exampleTranslation: The children came home with dirty clothes.

parts.homeLangDetails: A consonant-doubling transition that appears at morpheme boundaries in Finnish. It has no independent meaning but is part of the adjectival suffix attachment.
A very common Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'having the quality of' or 'characterized by.' Like English -ful or -ous, it turns a noun into an adjective: vaara (danger) → vaarallinen (dangerous).

words.exampleSentence: Tämä tie on hyvin vaarallinen talvella.
words.exampleTranslation: This road is very dangerous in winter.

parts.homeLangDetails: A consonant-doubling transition at the morpheme boundary before the adjectival suffix -inen. It carries no independent meaning.
A common Finnish adjectival suffix meaning 'having the quality of.' Here: turva (safety) → turvallinen (safe, secure).

words.exampleTranslation: Children can play in a safe environment.
It is important to eat healthily.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ton/-tön is Finnish's equivalent of English -less, meaning 'without' or 'lacking.' It attaches to a noun to form an adjective: merkitys (meaning) → merkityksetön (meaningless). The stem vowel shifts slightly for phonological harmony.

words.exampleSentence: Hän sanoi, että väittely oli täysin merkityksetön.
words.exampleTranslation: He said that the argument was completely meaningless.

parts.homeLangDetails: From opiskella (to study). The stem opiskele- carries the full meaning of studying or pursuing learning, often in a formal context like university.

The suffix -u/-y is a common Finnish nominalizing suffix that turns a verb into a noun denoting the action or process itself. Like English -ing in 'studying,' it makes the activity into a thing: opiskella (to study) → opiskelu (studying, the act of studying).

words.exampleSentence: Opiskelu yliopistossa on haastavaa mutta palkitsevaa.
words.exampleTranslation: Studying at university is challenging but rewarding.

parts.homeLangDetails: From harjoitella (to practice, to train). The root harjoite- relates to practicing or exercising a skill, and the -tte- is part of the frequentative/iterative verb stem, suggesting repeated or ongoing action.
The suffix -lu/-ly nominalizes the verb, turning the action of practicing into a noun: harjoitella (to practice) → harjoittelu (practicing, training, internship).

words.exampleTranslation: Practice makes perfect.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -tu/-ty here is part of the reflexive/passive verb formation, making valmistua mean 'to become ready' or 'to graduate' — something that happens to or within the subject, rather than something they do to an external object.

The suffix -minen is the most common Finnish verbal noun suffix, equivalent to English -ing or -tion. It turns any verb into a noun representing the action: valmistua (to graduate/finish) → valmistuminen (graduating, completion, graduation).

words.exampleTranslation: Graduating from university is a great achievement.

parts.homeLangDetails: From aloittaa (to start, to begin), a causative/transitive verb built on alku (beginning). The aloitta- stem carries the active sense of initiating something.
The suffix -minen turns a verb into a noun of action. Here: aloittaa (to start) → aloittaminen (starting, the act of beginning).

words.exampleSentence: Uuden projektin aloittaminen on aina jännittävää.
words.exampleTranslation: Starting a new project is always exciting.

parts.homeLangDetails: From lopettaa (to stop, to finish, to end), built on loppu (end, finish). The -tta- marks this as a causative form, meaning to bring something to an end.
The suffix -minen nominalizes the verb: lopettaa (to stop) → lopettaminen (stopping, ending, quitting).

words.exampleSentence: Tupakoinnin lopettaminen on vaikeaa mutta mahdollista.
words.exampleTranslation: Quitting smoking is difficult but possible.

parts.homeLangDetails: From muuttaa (to change something, or to move/relocate). This verb can mean both transforming something and moving to a new place — context tells you which. The -tta- marks it as a transitive/causative form.
The suffix -minen turns muuttaa into a noun: muuttaminen (changing, moving, the act of relocating or transforming).

words.exampleSentence: Muuttaminen uuteen kaupunkiin on iso päätös.
words.exampleTranslation: Moving to a new city is a big decision.

parts.homeLangDetails: From rakentaa (to build, to construct). Related to rakennus (building) and rakenne (structure). The -nta-/-nna- element is part of the verb stem.
The suffix -minen nominalizes the verb: rakentaa (to build) → rakentaminen (building, construction, the act of constructing).

words.exampleTranslation: Building the house took two years.

parts.homeLangDetails: From rakentaa (to build), with the causative/commissive suffix -uta/-utta- embedded in the stem. Rakennuttaa means 'to have something built' or 'to commission construction' — you're the one who makes building happen, not necessarily the one swinging the hammer.

The -tta/-ttä suffix creates a causative verb — one who causes or commissions an action rather than doing it directly. Rakennuttaa means 'to have (something) built,' distinguishing the developer/commissioner from the builder.

The suffix -ja/-jä is the standard Finnish agent suffix, equivalent to English -er. It turns a verb into a person who does that action: rakennuttaa (to commission building) → rakennuttaja (developer, one who commissions construction).

words.exampleTranslation: The developer commissioned plans for a new residential building.

parts.homeLangDetails: From myydä (to sell). The stem myy- is the core of the verb.
The suffix -ja/-jä is the Finnish agent suffix, meaning 'one who does X.' Here: myydä (to sell) → myyjä (seller, salesperson).

words.exampleSentence: Myyjä auttoi minua löytämään oikean koon.
words.exampleTranslation: The salesperson helped me find the right size.

parts.homeLangDetails: From ostaa (to buy). The stem osta- carries the full meaning of purchasing.
The suffix -ja/-jä forms agent nouns: ostaa (to buy) → ostaja (buyer, purchaser).

words.exampleTranslation: The buyer paid the full price for the house.

parts.homeLangDetails: From tuottaa (to produce, to yield, to generate). Related to tuote (product) and tuotos (output). The -tta- marks this as a causative/transitive verb form.
The suffix -ja/-jä forms agent nouns: tuottaa (to produce) → tuottaja (producer, one who produces).

words.exampleTranslation: The film producer presented the project to the investors.

parts.homeLangDetails: From kulua (to wear down, to be consumed, to pass). The same root appears in kulua (to pass/wear), kulutus (consumption), and kuluttaa (to consume/spend). Think of something gradually used up.
The -tta-/-ta- suffix makes a verb causative — here turning 'wear down' into 'cause to wear down / consume.' Combined with the following -ja, it forms the base for the agent noun.

words.exampleSentence: Kuluttaja voi valita itse, mitä ostaa.
words.exampleTranslation: The consumer can choose for themselves what to buy.

parts.homeLangDetails: From johtaa (to lead, to manage, to conduct). The root johta- also appears in johto (leadership, cable), johdanto (introduction), and johtopäätös (conclusion). Think of guiding something along a path.

words.exampleTranslation: The leader gave a long speech at the meeting.

parts.homeLangDetails: From päättää (to end, to decide) + -ös (noun-forming suffix). The idea is 'bringing something to an end' — reaching a conclusion. Related: pää (head/end), päättyä (to conclude).
A genitive linking element that connects the two halves of this compound, meaning roughly 'of decisions.' Finnish compound nouns often use the genitive case to join their parts.
From tehdä (to do, to make). Teko on its own means 'act' or 'deed.' The same root appears in tekijä (doer, author), tekoäly (artificial intelligence), and teos (work/piece).

words.exampleSentence: Päätöksenteko voi olla vaikeaa epävarmoissa tilanteissa.
words.exampleTranslation: Decision-making can be difficult in uncertain situations.

parts.homeLangDetails: From yhteinen (common, shared, joint), itself from yhte- (one/together). This prefix appears in many compound words: yhteiskunta (society), yhteisö (community), yhteinen (common). Think of 'what is held together by all.'

words.exampleTranslation: The project succeeded thanks to good cooperation.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -lainen/-läinen is one of Finnish's most productive suffixes, forming nouns that mean 'a person belonging to or associated with' something. Here: a person of the people/nation, i.e. a citizen. The same suffix forms nationalities and regional identities.

words.exampleSentence: Jokainen kansalainen on tasa-arvoinen lain edessä.
words.exampleTranslation: Every citizen is equal before the law.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root Suomi (Finland) in its stem form. The origin of the word Suomi is debated, but it is the Finnish name for Finland itself. Here the stem appears as suoma- before the suffix.

words.translationLong: a Finn, Finnish person; also used as adjective meaning 'Finnish'
words.exampleTranslation: She is a Finnish author.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of Ruotsi, the Finnish name for Sweden. The origin likely relates to Old Norse 'Roþs-' referring to the coastal rowing people. Here the stem appears as ruotsa- before the suffix.

words.translationLong: a Swede, Swedish person; also used as adjective meaning 'Swedish'
words.exampleTranslation: Our Swedish neighbor speaks fluent Finnish.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of Saksa, the Finnish name for Germany. It derives from the name of the Saxon people (Saxons), who were among the most prominent Germanic groups encountered by early Finns and Baltic peoples.

words.translationLong: a German, German person; also used as adjective meaning 'German'
words.exampleTranslation: A German engineer designed the bridge.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of Ranska, the Finnish name for France. It derives from the Franks (Franchi), the Germanic people who gave their name to France. Here the stem appears as ranska- before the suffix.

words.translationLong: a French person; also used as adjective meaning 'French'
words.exampleSentence: Ranskalainen kollega opetti minulle muutaman sanonnan.
words.exampleTranslation: A French colleague taught me a few sayings.

words.translationLong: a European; also used as adjective meaning 'European'
words.exampleTranslation: European culture is very diverse.

parts.homeLangDetails: A genitive linking element connecting the two parts of the compound, meaning roughly 'of nations.' Finnish compounds frequently use the genitive to join their components.
Väli means 'space between,' 'gap,' or 'interval.' It appears in many compounds: väliaika (halftime/interval), välimatka (distance), välitön (immediate/direct). Here it captures the 'inter-' idea.

The suffix -nen/-inen is extremely common in Finnish for forming adjectives from nouns. It means roughly 'of the nature of' or 'relating to.' Here it turns the noun compound into the adjective 'international.'

words.exampleSentence: Sopimus on tärkeä kansainvälisen rauhan kannalta.
words.exampleTranslation: The agreement is important for international peace.

parts.homeLangDetails: Ympäri means 'around' or 'all around.' It appears in words like ympäröidä (to surround), ympyrä (circle), and ympäriinsä (all around/everywhere). It gives a vivid sense of what encircles you.

The suffix -stö/-sto forms collective or abstract nouns from stems — often meaning 'a collection of' or 'the totality of' something. Compare: kalusto (equipment/fleet), henkilöstö (staff/personnel), kalasto (fish stock). Here it turns 'around' into 'the totality of what surrounds,' i.e. the environment.

words.exampleSentence: Meidän täytyy suojella ympäristöä tulevien sukupolvien takia.
words.exampleTranslation: We must protect the environment for future generations.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive suffix -n linking the two parts of the compound, meaning 'of the environment.' A very common pattern in Finnish compound nouns.

From suojella (to protect) + -u (noun-forming suffix). Suojella itself comes from suoja (shelter, protection). The same root appears in suojelualue (protected area), suoja (cover/shelter), and suojavarusteet (protective gear).

words.translationLong: environmental protection, conservation of the environment
words.exampleSentence: Ympäristönsuojelu on yksi tärkeimmistä poliittisista aiheista.
words.exampleTranslation: Environmental protection is one of the most important political topics.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of luonto (nature). The genitive -n here links the two compound parts, meaning 'of nature.' Luonto appears in luonnonvara (natural resource), luonnontiede (natural science), and luonnonmukainen (natural/organic).

words.exampleTranslation: Nature conservation is important for preserving biodiversity.

parts.homeLangDetails: The genitive form of ilmasto (climate), itself from ilma (air) + -sto (collective suffix). The genitive -n links the parts, meaning 'of the climate.' Compare ympäristö: just as that word is built on 'surrounding,' ilmasto is literally 'the totality of the air.'

From muuttaa (to change, to move) + -os/-ös (noun-forming suffix meaning 'the result or act of'). The same root appears in muuttaa (to change/relocate), muutos (change/amendment), and muuttuja (variable). A muutos is a concrete change or alteration.

words.exampleSentence: Ilmastonmuutos on yksi aikamme suurimmista haasteista.
words.exampleTranslation: Climate change is one of the greatest challenges of our time.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root saas- or saast- relates to dirt, filth, or contamination. It appears in words like saastua (to become polluted) and saaste (pollutant).
This -u- is a Finnish inchoative/intransitive suffix, turning the root into a verb meaning 'to become polluted' (saastua). It signals that the subject undergoes the change rather than causing it.
The -minen suffix turns a verb into a noun, like English '-ing' or '-tion'. Saastua (to become polluted) + -minen = the act or process of becoming polluted.

words.translationLong: the process of becoming polluted or contaminated
words.exampleTranslation: The pollution of waterways is a serious environmental problem.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root kierr-, related to kiertää (to go around, to circulate). The same root appears in kierros (round, loop) and kierros (a turn or cycle).

The -ättä- is a causative suffix meaning 'to make something go around' or 'to cause circulation'. It transforms the intransitive root into a transitive, causative verb: kierrättää (to recycle, literally 'to make go around again').
The -minen suffix turns the verb kierrättää into a noun: the act or process of recycling.

words.exampleTranslation: Recycling helps reduce the amount of waste.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root kasv- is the core of kasvaa (to grow). It appears in kasvatus (upbringing, cultivation), kasvi (plant), and kasvisto (flora).
The -a here is part of the infinitive ending of kasvaa. When -minen attaches, the infinitive vowel is retained as part of the verbal noun stem.
The -minen suffix turns the verb kasvaa (to grow) into a noun: the act or process of growing.

words.exampleTranslation: A child's growing happens quickly.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root kehit-, related to kehittää (to develop, to advance). The same root appears in kehitys (development) and kehittyä (to develop oneself/naturally).

The -ty- is a reflexive or medio-passive suffix, turning the causative kehittää (to develop something) into kehittyä (to develop on one's own, to evolve naturally). It signals that the subject undergoes the process rather than initiating it externally.
The -minen suffix turns kehittyä (to develop) into a noun: the process of developing or evolving.

words.translationLong: the process of developing or evolving
words.exampleTranslation: Professional development requires continuous studying.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root opp-, the core of oppia (to learn). Related words include oppi (learning, doctrine), oppilas (student), opettaa (to teach), and opettaja (teacher).
The -i- is a stem vowel that connects the root to the -minen suffix. It is part of the infinitive stem of oppia.
The -minen suffix turns oppia (to learn) into a noun: the act or process of learning.

words.translationLong: the act or process of learning
words.exampleSentence: Kielen oppiminen vie aikaa mutta on palkitsevaa.
words.exampleTranslation: Learning a language takes time but is rewarding.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ymmärt- is the core of ymmärtää (to understand). It appears in ymmärrys (understanding, comprehension) and ymmärrettävä (understandable). The origin is somewhat obscure but the root is distinctively Finnish.
The -ä- is the stem vowel of the infinitive ymmärtää, retained when -minen attaches to form the verbal noun.
The -minen suffix turns ymmärtää (to understand) into a noun: the act or process of understanding.

words.translationLong: the act or process of understanding
words.exampleSentence: Toisen ihmisen ymmärtäminen on tärkeä taito.
words.exampleTranslation: Understanding another person is an important skill.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root muist- is the core of muistaa (to remember). Related words include muisti (memory), muistio (memo), muistiinpano (note), and muistella (to reminisce).
The -a- is the stem vowel of the infinitive muistaa, retained when -minen attaches.
The -minen suffix turns muistaa (to remember) into a noun: the act of remembering.

words.exampleTranslation: Remembering names is difficult for me.

words.flag: Possible typo: standard Finnish spelling is 'unohtaminen' (single t). Entry created as given but note the likely misspelling.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root unoh- is the core of unohtaa (to forget). Related words include unohtunut (forgotten) and unohdus (forgetfulness, an instance of forgetting). The root may be distantly related to uni (sleep/dream), suggesting a sense of something 'slipping away'.
The -tta- is a causative suffix. In unohtaa it contributes to the transitive sense of actively forgetting something, as opposed to something slipping from memory passively.
The -minen suffix turns unohtaa (to forget) into a noun: the act of forgetting.

words.exampleSentence: Tärkeiden asioiden unohtaminen voi olla stressaavaa.
words.exampleTranslation: Forgetting important things can be stressful.

parts.homeLangDetails: Think of this as Finnish's version of "un-" or "dis-". It flips a word to its opposite, and it's incredibly common. You'll see it in epäonninen (unlucky), epäselvä (unclear), and epätodellinen (unreal). Once you spot it, you'll start seeing it everywhere.

The noun form of onnistua (to succeed), itself built on onni (luck, happiness) + -stua (a reflexive/inchoative verb suffix). So 'onnistuminen' is literally 'a coming into luck' — the act of succeeding. The epä- prefix flips it to failure.

words.translationLong: failure, a failing, an instance of not succeeding
words.exampleTranslation: Failure is part of learning.

parts.homeLangDetails: Think of this as Finnish's version of "un-" or "dis-". It flips a word to its opposite, and it's incredibly common. You'll see it in epäonninen (unlucky), epäselvä (unclear), and epätodellinen (unreal). Once you spot it, you'll start seeing it everywhere.
From selväs/selvä, meaning clear, distinct, or sober. The same root appears in selvittää (to clarify) and selvyys (clarity).
The -sti adverb suffix attaches to adjectives to form manner adverbs — here to epäselvä (unclear), giving 'unclearly'. The -ä- before -sti is vowel harmony with the front vowels in the word.

words.exampleSentence: Hän puhui niin epäselvästi, että en ymmärtänyt mitään.
words.exampleTranslation: He spoke so unclearly that I didn't understand anything.

parts.homeLangDetails: Think of this as Finnish's version of "un-" or "dis-". It flips a word to its opposite, and it's incredibly common. You'll see it in epäonninen (unlucky), epäselvä (unclear), and epätodellinen (unreal). Once you spot it, you'll start seeing it everywhere.

From rehellis-, the stem of rehellinen (honest). Rehellinen itself comes from rehtä (straight, upright), with -llinen adding the sense of 'having the quality of'. The root rehtä is related to the concept of straightness or uprightness.

This is one of Finnish's most productive suffixes, roughly equivalent to English "-en", "-ish", or "-al". It turns nouns into adjectives. Puinen means wooden (from puu, tree), suomalainen means Finnish, and keltainen means yellow. You will meet this suffix constantly.

words.exampleTranslation: It is dishonest to lie to your friend.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix puts something before or in front of something else, much like English "pre-" or "fore-". It shows up in esimerkki (example, literally "fore-mark"), esimies (supervisor, literally "fore-man"), and esikoulu (preschool). It's a tidy little time-and-order marker.

words.exampleTranslation: The child starts preschool next autumn.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix puts something before or in front of something else, much like English "pre-" or "fore-". It shows up in esimerkki (example, literally "fore-mark"), esimies (supervisor, literally "fore-man"), and esikoulu (preschool). It's a tidy little time-and-order marker.

Mies means 'man' or 'person' in Finnish. In compounds like esimies it often carries a gender-neutral sense of 'person in a role', though the word itself is grammatically masculine in origin. Think of it like 'foreman' — the original meaning is gendered, but the usage has broadened.

words.exampleTranslation: My supervisor is very encouraging.

parts.homeLangDetails: This one works just like English "self-" in compounds. It comes from the word itse meaning "self" or "oneself". You'll find it in itseluottamus (self-confidence), itseopiskelu (self-study), and itsepalvelu (self-service). Very recognizable once you know itse on its own.

From luottaa (to trust) + -mus (abstract noun suffix). Luottaa itself comes from a root related to relying or leaning on something. The same root appears in luottokelpoisuus (creditworthiness) and luottamus (trust between people).

words.exampleTranslation: She has a lot of self-confidence.

parts.homeLangDetails: This one works just like English "self-" in compounds. It comes from the word itse meaning "self" or "oneself". You'll find it in itseluottamus (self-confidence), itseopiskelu (self-study), and itsepalvelu (self-service). Very recognizable once you know itse on its own.

The noun form of opiskella (to study), built on oppia (to learn) + -skella (a frequentative/continuative verb suffix). So opiskelu is the ongoing activity of studying — not just learning in a flash, but sustained engagement. See also: opiskelija (student).

words.exampleTranslation: Self-study requires a lot of discipline.

parts.homeLangDetails: This one works just like English "self-" in compounds. It comes from the word itse meaning "self" or "oneself". You'll find it in itseluottamus (self-confidence), itseopiskelu (self-study), and itsepalvelu (self-service). Very recognizable once you know itse on its own.

From määrätä (to determine, to order, to decree) + -mis (a nominalizing suffix forming verbal nouns, similar to -minen but used in compound contexts). Määrätä itself comes from määrä (amount, measure, limit). The sense here is 'the act of determining one's own course'.

From oikea (right, correct, straight) + -us (abstract noun suffix). Oikeus means both 'a right' (as in a legal entitlement) and 'justice' or 'court' depending on context. Here it means 'the right to'.

words.exampleTranslation: The right to self-determination is an important human right.

parts.homeLangDetails: Built on yhte- (together, joint) + -isö (a suffix forming collective or institutional nouns, similar to -sto/-stö). Yhteisö literally means 'a togetherness-group' — a community or collective. See also: yhteinen (common, shared).

A very common suffix that creates adjectives meaning "characterized by" or "having the quality of", similar to English "-ful", "-ly", or "-al". Rakastavainen means loving, onnellinen means happy (onni, luck/happiness), and tavallinen means ordinary. It adds a sense of "full of" or "in the manner of".

words.exampleSentence: Meillä on vahva yhteisöllinen henki tässä kylässä.
words.exampleTranslation: We have a strong communal spirit in this village.

parts.homeLangDetails: The combining form of yhteinen (common, shared, joint), itself from yhte- (together). This form appears in many Finnish compounds: yhteiskunta (society), yhteistyö (cooperation), yhteisvastuu. The -s at the end is a genitive/compound linking element.

From vastata (to respond, to be responsible for) + -uu (a suffix forming abstract nouns). Vastuu is the noun for responsibility or liability. The same root appears in vastaus (answer) and vastuullinen (responsible).

words.translationLong: joint responsibility, collective responsibility, shared liability
words.exampleSentence: Yhteisvastuu tarkoittaa, että kaikki vastaavat yhdessä.
words.exampleTranslation: Joint responsibility means that everyone is responsible together.

parts.homeLangDetails: This root meaning "book" or "writing" is one of the most useful in Finnish. Kirja means book, kirjasto is library, kirjain is a letter of the alphabet, and kirjoittaa means to write. It is distantly related to the same Old Germanic roots that give English "script" its flavor, and it seeds a whole family of literacy-related words.

The -ili- here is part of the verb kirjailla (to embroider, to embellish with writing), a frequentative/ornamental verb derived from kirja. In the context of kirjailija, this verb form lends the sense of 'one who works with writing in a creative or elaborate way' — a writer or author, not just someone who writes functionally.

This is Finnish's answer to English "-er" as in "someone who does X". It attaches to verb stems to make nouns for people or things that perform an action. A laulaja is a singer (laulaa, to sing), an opettaja is a teacher (opettaa, to teach), and an ajaja is a driver. Super consistent and very handy.

words.exampleTranslation: A well-known author published a new novel.

parts.homeLangDetails: This root meaning "book" or "writing" is one of the most useful in Finnish. Kirja means book, kirjasto is library, kirjain is a letter of the alphabet, and kirjoittaa means to write. It is distantly related to the same Old Germanic roots that give English "script" its flavor, and it seeds a whole family of literacy-related words.

A double-l connector that appears when -llinen attaches to certain noun stems. It is part of the suffix complex and has no independent meaning, but it is required by Finnish phonological rules for this class of words.

This is one of Finnish's most productive suffixes, roughly equivalent to English "-en", "-ish", or "-al". It turns nouns into adjectives. Puinen means wooden (from puu, tree), suomalainen means Finnish, and keltainen means yellow. You will meet this suffix constantly.

words.exampleTranslation: He gave a written warning.

parts.homeLangDetails: From kirja (book, writing) with a diminutive/variant form kirje specifically meaning a written letter — a piece of correspondence. The same root but a slightly different form from kirja, reflecting the more specific sense of a letter sent to someone.

Kuori means shell, husk, peel, or outer covering — the outer layer of something. In kirjekuori it refers to the paper sleeve that holds a letter. The same word is used for an orange peel or a snail's shell.

words.exampleTranslation: Put the letter in the envelope before sending it.

parts.homeLangDetails: Talo means house, and this root appears across a big family of words about buildings and home life. Taloyhtiö is a housing company, talous means household or economy, and talvi (winter) is a separate word but talo itself is everywhere. It is a great anchor word for domestic vocabulary.

From yhte-/yhti- (together, joint) + -ö (a suffix forming collective or institutional nouns). Yhtiö means a company or corporation — literally 'a togetherness entity'. A taloyhtiö is the legal entity that jointly owns and manages a residential building, similar to a housing cooperative or homeowners' association.

words.translationLong: housing company, residential building cooperative, homeowners' association
words.exampleTranslation: The housing company decided to renovate all the balconies.

parts.homeLangDetails: From talo (house, building) + -us (abstract noun suffix). Originally meant household management — the running of a home or estate. This is exactly the same semantic path as the Greek oikonomia (eco-nomy): oikos (house) + nomos (management). Now used broadly for economy and economics.

From tietää (to know) + a nominalizing suffix. Tiede means science or an academic discipline — systematic knowledge. It appears in many Finnish compound discipline names: lääketiede (medicine), oikeustiede (law), luonnontiede (natural science).

words.exampleTranslation: She studies economics at university.

parts.homeLangDetails: From talo- (house, building) with the -us suffix forming an abstract noun. The original sense is 'household management' — the same root as the Greek oikos in 'economics'. Here it means the national economy broadly.

words.exampleTranslation: The government published a new economic policy plan.

parts.homeLangDetails: Työ means work, and it anchors a huge cluster of everyday words. Työtä tehdä means to do work, työpaikka is a workplace, and työttömyys means unemployment. There is no direct English cognate, but once you know työ you will start seeing it in nearly every conversation about daily life.

A fusion of -tön/-ttön (without, lacking) and -ys (abstract noun suffix). Together they turn an adjective like 'workless' into the noun 'worklessness' — i.e., the state of lacking work. The double-t reflects Finnish consonant gradation.

words.exampleTranslation: Unemployment has decreased in recent years.

parts.homeLangDetails: Työ means work, and it anchors a huge cluster of everyday words. Työtä tehdä means to do work, työpaikka is a workplace, and työttömyys means unemployment. There is no direct English cognate, but once you know työ you will start seeing it in nearly every conversation about daily life.
A fusion of -tön/-ttön (without, lacking) and -ys (abstract noun suffix) — together meaning 'the state of lacking work'. The double-t reflects Finnish consonant gradation.

words.translationLong: unemployment benefit, unemployment security, social protection for the unemployed
words.exampleSentence: Hän sai työttömyysturvaa kuuden kuukauden ajan.
words.exampleTranslation: She received unemployment benefit for six months.

parts.homeLangDetails: Työ (work, labor) in the genitive case (-n), linking it to the following element. In Finnish compounds the genitive signals 'giver of work' — the one who provides work.
From antaa (to give). This is the stem of the giving action — the same root appears in words like lahja-nantaja (gift-giver).

This is Finnish's answer to English "-er" as in "someone who does X". It attaches to verb stems to make nouns for people or things that perform an action. A laulaja is a singer (laulaa, to sing), an opettaja is a teacher (opettaa, to teach), and an ajaja is a driver. Super consistent and very handy.

words.exampleTranslation: The employer offered him a full-time employment contract.

parts.homeLangDetails: Työ (work, labor) in the genitive case (-n), linking it to the following element. The genitive here marks what is being sought: 'seeker of work'.
From hakea (to seek, to fetch, to apply for). This is the verb stem used in the agent noun construction. The same root appears in hakemisto (index, directory) and hakea töitä (to look for work).

This is Finnish's answer to English "-er" as in "someone who does X". It attaches to verb stems to make nouns for people or things that perform an action. A laulaja is a singer (laulaa, to sing), an opettaja is a teacher (opettaa, to teach), and an ajaja is a driver. Super consistent and very handy.

words.exampleTranslation: The job seeker sent their application by email.

parts.homeLangDetails: Päivä means day and seeds a rich family of time and greeting words. Tänään means today, huomenna means tomorrow, but päivä itself appears in hyvää päivää (good day), arkipäivä (weekday, literally "everyday day"), and päiväkoti (daycare, literally "day home"). A foundational root for talking about time.

words.exampleTranslation: The child is very happy at daycare.

parts.homeLangDetails: Päivä means day and seeds a rich family of time and greeting words. Tänään means today, huomenna means tomorrow, but päivä itself appears in hyvää päivää (good day), arkipäivä (weekday, literally "everyday day"), and päiväkoti (daycare, literally "day home"). A foundational root for talking about time.

It is distantly related to the same Old Germanic roots that give English "script" its flavor, and it seeds a whole family of literacy-related words.

words.exampleTranslation: She writes in her diary every evening.

parts.homeLangDetails: Vesi means water, and its stem vete- appears when suffixes are added. Vesijohto is a water pipe, vedenalainen means underwater, and vedenpitävä means waterproof. The stem shift from vesi to vete is a classic Finnish pattern, and spotting this root makes water-related vocabulary click immediately.

words.exampleSentence: Kaupungin vanha vesitorni on nyt museo.
words.exampleTranslation: The city's old water tower is now a museum.

parts.homeLangDetails: Vesi means water, and its stem vete- appears when suffixes are added. Vesijohto is a water pipe, vedenalainen means underwater, and vedenpitävä means waterproof. The stem shift from vesi to vete is a classic Finnish pattern, and spotting this root makes water-related vocabulary click immediately.

From laitaa/laittaa (to set up, to arrange), with the -os suffix forming a noun. A laitos is an established facility or institution — the same word is used for a hospital (sairaalaitos), a university (laitos as 'department'), or any public utility.

words.translationLong: waterworks, water utility, water treatment plant
words.exampleTranslation: The water utility takes care of the distribution of clean water.

parts.homeLangDetails: Think of this as Finnish's version of "over-" or "super-." It stacks onto nouns and adjectives to mean something exceeds a limit or sits above something else. You'll spot it in ylinopeus (speeding, literally "over-speed"), ylipaino (overweight), and yliopisto (university, literally "over-school," the highest level of study).

From oppia (to learn), with -isto forming a noun meaning a place of learning. An opisto is a school or institute; yliopisto adds yli- (over, above) to mean the highest level of learning institution — a university.

This suffix turns a place name into a word for the people or things from that place, just like English "-ian" or "-ese." Suomi gives you suomalainen (Finnish person), Helsinki gives helsinkiläinen (Helsinkian), and Eurooppa gives eurooppalainen (European). It also carries cultural identity, so it's a high-frequency word you'll need from day one.

words.translationLong: university student, member of a university community
words.exampleSentence: Hän on ylioppilas, mutta hänen sisarensa on yliopistolainen.
words.exampleTranslation: He is a high school graduate, but his sister is a university student.

parts.homeLangDetails: Think of this as Finnish's version of "over-" or "super-." It stacks onto nouns and adjectives to mean something exceeds a limit or sits above something else. You'll spot it in ylinopeus (speeding, literally "over-speed"), ylipaino (overweight), and yliopisto (university, literally "over-school," the highest level of study).

From kuorma (load, cargo, burden). The stem kuormi- appears in verbs like kuormittaa (to burden, to load) and nouns like kuormitus (loading, strain). The same root gives us kuorma-auto (truck, literally 'load-car').
A variant of the -us/-ys suffix that forms abstract nouns from verbs, especially causative or action verbs. Here kuormittaa (to load/burden) → kuormitus (the act or state of loading/burdening).

words.exampleTranslation: Network overload slows down the connection.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to yli-, this works just like English "under-" to mean something is below a threshold or subordinate. It shows up in alipaino (underweight), aliarvioida (to underestimate), and alisuorittaa (to underperform). Once you know the yli-/ali- pair, a whole family of words clicks into place.

From suoriutua (to perform, to manage, to get through something). The root suori- relates to suora (straight, direct) — suoriutua originally means to straighten out or get through a task. Here it carries the sense of performing or achieving.

Finnish turns verbs into nouns by adding -minen, similar to English "-ing" as a gerund. Syöminen means eating (syödä, to eat), juominen means drinking, and nukkuminen means sleeping. These nouns are everywhere in conversation and writing.

words.exampleTranslation: The teacher became concerned about the student's underperformance.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to yli-, this works just like English "under-" to mean something is below a threshold or subordinate. It shows up in alipaino (underweight), aliarvioida (to underestimate), and alisuorittaa (to underperform). Once you know the yli-/ali- pair, a whole family of words clicks into place.

This is one of Finnish's most productive suffixes, roughly equivalent to English "-en", "-ish", or "-al". It turns nouns into adjectives. Puinen means wooden (from puu, tree), suomalainen means Finnish, and keltainen means yellow. You will meet this suffix constantly.

words.exampleTranslation: Many healthcare workers are underpaid.

parts.homeLangDetails: Pää literally means "head," and it works just like "head-" or "main-" in English compounds. The learner's best friends here are pääkaupunki (capital city, "head city"), pääasia (the main point), and pääministeri (prime minister). It's one of the most productive compounding elements in everyday Finnish.

words.exampleTranslation: Helsinki is the capital of Finland.

parts.homeLangDetails: Pää literally means "head," and it works just like "head-" or "main-" in English compounds. The learner's best friends here are pääkaupunki (capital city, "head city"), pääasia (the main point), and pääministeri (prime minister). It's one of the most productive compounding elements in everyday Finnish.

words.exampleTranslation: The prime minister held a press conference yesterday evening.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root uus-/uude- (new). Here it forms the base of a verb meaning 'to renew/reform'. The stem shifts slightly to 'uudi-' before the verb-forming elements.

The -stu/-sty suffix makes a verb reflexive or intransitive — indicating that the subject undergoes the change rather than causing it in something else. So uudistua means 'to renew oneself, to be renewed'.

Finnish turns verbs into nouns by adding -minen, similar to English "-ing" as a gerund. Syöminen means eating (syödä, to eat), juominen means drinking, and nukkuminen means sleeping. These nouns are everywhere in conversation and writing.

words.exampleTranslation: The company's renewal was necessary.

parts.homeLangDetails: From uus-/uude- (new). The form uudis- appears specifically in compounds referring to something newly created or pioneering, like a new settlement or new construction.
From rakentaa (to build). Rakennus means both the act of building and the resulting structure — context usually makes clear which is meant. Here it refers to the physical structure.

words.exampleTranslation: The new building was completed on schedule.

parts.homeLangDetails: Etu means both "front" and "benefit/advantage," so context matters, but in compounds it usually marks position or priority. You'll see it in etunimi (first name, literally "front name"), etuovi (front door), and etuoikeus (privilege, "front right"). It overlaps nicely with English "pre-" and "front-."
From oikeus (right, justice, law). The stem oikeu- appears here before the -tettu suffix. Oikeus itself comes from oikea (right, correct).
This is a past passive participle suffix (-ttu/-tty after a long stem). So etuoikeutettu literally means 'one to whom a prior right has been granted' — privileged.

words.exampleTranslation: She was born into a privileged family.

parts.homeLangDetails: Etu means both "front" and "benefit/advantage," so context matters, but in compounds it usually marks position or priority. You'll see it in etunimi (first name, literally "front name"), etuovi (front door), and etuoikeus (privilege, "front right"). It overlaps nicely with English "pre-" and "front-."

Rivi means 'row' or 'line'. The form rivin is the genitive (possessive) case — so eturivin means 'of the front row'. It is typically used as a modifier: eturivin poliitikko = a front-row (i.e. leading) politician.

words.exampleTranslation: She is a leading expert in her field.

parts.homeLangDetails: From ilma (air, atmosphere) + -sto (a suffix forming collective or systemic nouns). So ilmasto is literally 'the air-system' — the overall atmospheric condition, i.e. climate.

A very common suffix that creates adjectives meaning "characterized by" or "having the quality of", similar to English "-ful", "-ly", or "-al". Rakastavainen means loving, onnellinen means happy (onni, luck/happiness), and tavallinen means ordinary. It adds a sense of "full of" or "in the manner of".

words.exampleTranslation: Climatic changes affect the whole world.

parts.homeLangDetails: Luonto means 'nature' (in the sense of the natural world) and also 'character, disposition'. The stem shifts to luonnol- before the -llinen suffix through consonant gradation and assimilation.

A very common suffix that creates adjectives meaning "characterized by" or "having the quality of", similar to English "-ful", "-ly", or "-al". Rakastavainen means loving, onnellinen means happy (onni, luck/happiness), and tavallinen means ordinary. It adds a sense of "full of" or "in the manner of".

words.exampleTranslation: It is natural that children play.

parts.homeLangDetails: Luonto means 'nature' or 'character'. The stem shifts to luonnollis- through consonant gradation and assimilation before the abstract noun suffix.

This is -llinen + -uus merged in sequence: the adjectival suffix -llinen (having the quality of) combined with -uus (abstract noun, state or result). Together they convert the noun luonto into an abstract quality: naturalness.

words.exampleTranslation: There was a unique naturalness in her speech.

words.exampleTranslation: Sauna culture is considered part of Finnish identity.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of 'ruotsi', the Finnish word for Sweden/Swedish. The same root appears in 'ruotsalainen' (a Swedish person).

The suffix -lainen/-läinen attaches to place or people names to form demonyms or adjectives of origin. Here it derives the adjective/noun 'ruotsalainen' (Swedish, a Swede) before the abstract suffix is added.

Uusi means "new," and in compounds it behaves a bit like English "re-" or "neo-." Watch for uusvuosi (New Year), uusintа (repeat, rerun), and uudelleen (again, anew). The stem shifts to uude- in some forms, but the meaning stays rock solid.

words.translationLong: Swedish identity, the quality of being Swedish
words.exampleTranslation: She reflected on the meaning of Swedishness for her identity.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of 'kieli' (language, tongue). The same root appears in 'kielioppi' (grammar) and 'kielitaito' (language proficiency).

A linking or stem-extension element that appears between the root and the -inen suffix in many Finnish adjective derivations. It doesn't carry independent meaning but is required for the word to sound natural.

This is one of Finnish's most productive suffixes, roughly equivalent to English "-en", "-ish", or "-al". It turns nouns into adjectives. Puinen means wooden (from puu, tree), suomalainen means Finnish, and keltainen means yellow. You will meet this suffix constantly.

words.exampleTranslation: Children have a natural linguistic talent.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of 'kieli' (language, tongue). See also: kielellinen.

This element packages the adjective-forming suffix -ellinen together with a verbalizing -stua/-stya pattern into a single stem. The combined effect is 'become linguistic/language-like'. Finnish frequently builds multi-layer derivations like this before adding the verbal noun ending.

A fusion of the reflexive/inchoative verbal suffix -uminenn/-yminen, forming a verbal noun meaning 'the process of becoming'. Here the full ending is -yminen, indicating an intransitive 'becoming' process.

words.translationLong: the process of becoming expressed in language, linguistification
words.exampleSentence: Tunteiden kielellistyminen on tärkeä osa terapiaa.
words.exampleTranslation: The linguistification of emotions is an important part of therapy.
words.flag: complex layered derivation; 3 parts used but internal structure is deeply layered

parts.homeLangDetails: The root 'osa' means 'part' or 'share', but in verb form 'osata' it means 'to know how, to be able'. This word derives from the participial stem 'osaava' (able, competent), so the meaning is grounded in the verbal sense.
From the present active participle suffix -ava/-ävä, meaning 'one who does / doing'. 'Osaava' means 'competent, skilled'. The -a drops before -uus.

Uusi means "new," and in compounds it behaves a bit like English "re-" or "neo-." Watch for uusvuosi (New Year), uusintа (repeat, rerun), and uudelleen (again, anew). The stem shifts to uude- in some forms, but the meaning stays rock solid.

words.exampleTranslation: The applicant's competence convinced the recruiter.

parts.homeLangDetails: A linking element that appears in Finnish derivations between a noun stem and adjectival or abstract suffixes. 'Osallinen' (partaking, involved) is formed with -llinen on the root 'osa'. The double-l here is part of that intermediate adjectival stem.

A fusion of the adjectival suffix -inen and the abstract noun suffix -uus, yielding '-ness' or 'the quality of being'. Compare: 'osallinen' (adjective: involved) → 'osallisuus' (noun: involvement, participation).

words.exampleTranslation: Children's participation in decision-making is important.

parts.homeLangDetails: The privative suffix -ton/-tön (without, lacking) appears here in its geminated/consonant-gradated form -tt- before the abstract ending. The full underlying form is 'osa' + 'ton' → 'osaton' (having no share, excluded), which then takes -uus.
The abstract noun ending -uus/-yys attached to the privative adjective stem. The -o- is a stem vowel from the -ton suffix grade. Together -omuus signals 'the state of being without'.

words.translationLong: exclusion, marginalization, lack of share or participation
words.exampleTranslation: Exclusion from society can lead to marginalization.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of 'tunto' (feeling, sense, touch) or 'tunne' (emotion), here in the sense underlying 'tunnollinen' (conscientious). The moral sense 'conscience' is built on the idea of inner feeling or sensitivity.
Part of the -ollinen/-öllinen adjectival suffix complex, forming 'tunnollinen' (conscientious). This linking element connects the root to the abstract suffix.
Fusion of -inen and -uus, converting the adjective 'tunnollinen' into an abstract noun. See also: osallisuus.

words.exampleSentence: Hänen tunnollisuutensa työssä on kaikkien tiedossa.
words.exampleTranslation: His conscientiousness at work is known to everyone.

parts.homeLangDetails: The stem of 'tunto' (feeling, sense) or 'tunne' (emotion). In 'tunnollinen' the meaning has shifted toward moral inner sense — the kind of feeling that keeps you honest and dutiful, i.e. conscience.

A linking/extension element between the noun stem and the adjectival suffix -inen. It appears in several Finnish adjectives derived from feeling- or state-nouns (compare: 'vastuullinen' from 'vastuu').

This is one of Finnish's most productive suffixes, roughly equivalent to English "-en", "-ish", or "-al". It turns nouns into adjectives. Puinen means wooden (from puu, tree), suomalainen means Finnish, and keltainen means yellow. You will meet this suffix constantly.

words.exampleSentence: Hän on tunnollinen opiskelija, joka tekee aina läksynsä.
words.exampleTranslation: She is a conscientious student who always does her homework.
